content: Quality gates that block execution in interactive mode must convert blockers to explicit assumptions in autonomous/non-interactive mode — they must never deadlock. In spec-writer and planner prompts, unchecked required readiness items block plan_create in interactive mode (until resolved or explicitly waived), but in autonomous mode those same items become explicit Assumptions/Open Questions and execution continues with narrowed scope. Hard-blocking autonomous runs deadlocks existing chains like plan-and-build; always proceeding without surfacing gaps makes the rubric toothless. The asymmetry is the correct design: interactive = gate, autonomous = document-and-proceed.

The critical-assumption escalation threshold is fixed at 3 in spec-writer.md with an explicit human waiver path. When critical assumptions reach 3 in interactive mode, spec-writer must run one more clarification round before writing the spec — unless the human explicitly waives with 'proceed with assumptions'. Critical assumptions are those that change user-visible behavior, scope boundaries, existing-feature interaction, or acceptance criteria. The fixed threshold was chosen over project-configurable or feature-size-inferred thresholds because Plan A excludes framework/config work, a fixed value is visible, simple, and testable, and the waiver path prevents trapping interactive users in an unnecessary loop when they knowingly accept the assumptions.

The four-factor readiness rubric (Specificity, Constraints, Context, Success criteria) is the shared shell used in both spec-writer and planner readiness checks, but with role-tailored checklist items under each heading. Using identical headings makes the gate recognizable and auditable by humans across both phases; using tailored items prevents a generic checklist vague enough to block nothing. For spec-writer: Specificity checks purpose/user/happy-path/failure-flow; Constraints checks scope/existing-feature interactions; Context checks code/doc citations; Success criteria checks user-verifiable ACs with ≥⅓ error/edge coverage. For planner: same headings but items target architecture (module boundaries, dependency direction, integration seams, QC-rule reference).

Cosmo's planning router has three named paths — spec-writer, cosmo-facilitates-dialogue, and planner-autonomous — not two. Collapsing to a binary spec-writer/planner router drops the existing Cosmo-facilitated interactive design dialogue behavior already encoded at bundled/coding/coding/prompts/cosmo.md:37-40. The three signals are: (1) fuzzy/no-spec → spec-writer; (2) user wants interactive design dialogue or request is concrete enough for architecture back-and-forth → cosmo-facilitates-dialogue (Cosmo loads /skill:design-dialogue, walks frame→shape→detail in-session, then spawns planner); (3) 'just decide'/'go ahead'/'commit', non-interactive, or post-dialogue → planner-autonomous. Each route requires a visible route announcement with Route, Why, and Next fields before Cosmo proceeds.

The plan-reviewer caught that the initial plan proposed a binary spec-writer/planner router, misreading the existing cosmo.md contract. bundled/coding/coding/prompts/cosmo.md:37-40 already encodes three distinct planning behaviors — fuzzy work to spec-writer, interactive design dialogue stays in Cosmo itself, and autonomous planner only once direction is settled. A worker implementing the binary router literally would have silently dropped the Cosmo-facilitated interactive design dialogue path. Lesson: before planning changes to a prompt file, read the actual current prompt — the existing contract may be richer than the design doc assumes.

Readiness check blocks emitted before plan_create must remain conversational-only output — they must not become new persisted sections in spec.md or plan.md. The spec-writer output format persists exactly: Purpose, Users, User Experience, Acceptance Criteria, Scope, Assumptions, Open Questions. The planner output format (planner.md:218-248) must not gain a new Plan Readiness Check section. This boundary matters because downstream agents (planner, plan-reviewer) treat spec.md as the authoritative source of requirements; adding a Readiness Check section would change the artifact shape those agents consume. Enforce this boundary with a reviewer-verified QC criterion (not auto-tested) that inspects the persisted output format sections directly.

Prompt-contract tests use the readFile + toContain pattern to lock durable phrases, not snapshot full prompt files. Pattern: import readFile from node:fs/promises, resolve the prompt path with new URL(..., import.meta.url), read with utf-8 encoding, then assert expect(content).toContain('exact-phrase') in describe/it blocks. Assert section labels and behavioral key phrases (routing signals, gate behavior, waiver language, threshold wording) — not every line of copy. This lets prompt wording be refined without causing test churn while still preventing silent deletion of routing contracts, phase-transition announcements, blocking rules, and non-persisted-output boundaries. See tests/prompts/integration-verifier.test.ts and tests/prompts/quality-manager.test.ts as canonical examples.

When adding a pre-tool-call gate that references existing quality rules, the gate must point to the existing rule rather than restate it. planner.md step 5 already defines the QC rule (3-8 criteria, ≥⅓ failure/edge cases). The Plan Readiness Check block's Success criteria item says 'the QC section meets the rule already defined in step 5' rather than copying the counts. Adding a third independent copy of the same rule inside the same file creates silent drift risk: if the canonical rule changes, the gate copy does not update. The same principle applies anywhere in the codebase — references beat copies.

Phase cadence (Frame → Shape → Detail) for spec-writer belongs in spec-writer.md, not in the design-dialogue skill. bundled/coding/coding/skills/design-dialogue/SKILL.md:98-100 explicitly says requirements capture belongs to spec-writer, and bundled/coding/coding/agents/spec-writer.ts:9-11 shows spec-writer does not load that skill. Encoding the cadence in the persona prompt satisfies the plan without violating agent-definition scope or broadening a skill past its explicit boundaries. General rule: if a skill explicitly excludes a behavior, put that behavior in the agent's persona prompt, not in the skill.

Expanding cosmo.subagents in bundled/coding/coding/agents/cosmo.ts breaks the coding-agents invariant test (tests/domains/coding-agents.test.ts) if that test hardcodes expected agent IDs. The round-1 finding F-001 was triggered by cosmo.subagents referencing new agent IDs absent from the invariant fixture. The fix: have the invariant test derive expected definitions from the real coding-domain loader (loadDomainsFromSources) rather than a hardcoded list, so the test stays valid as the allowlist evolves. Whenever a new agent is added to any subagents allowlist, verify the invariant test loads the full live domain rather than asserting against a static snapshot.
